items.question: What is a pulse survey used for?
items.correctAnswer: Frequent short checks on morale, change readiness, or specific topics.
items.explanation: Pulse data must be acted on to build trust.

items.question: What is employee engagement commonly distinguished from?
items.correctAnswer: Satisfaction—engagement includes willingness to exert discretionary effort toward goals.
items.explanation: Satisfied employees may still disengage.

items.question: What is recognition’s role in engagement?
items.correctAnswer: Reinforces valued behaviors and helps people see impact beyond pay.
items.explanation: Timely specific recognition beats generic praise.

items.question: What workplace factors often contribute to burnout?
items.correctAnswer: Unsustainable workload, lack of control, insufficient reward, breakdown of community, unfairness, values mismatch.
items.explanation: Burnout is organizational, not only individual.

items.question: Why can manager quality heavily influence team engagement?
items.correctAnswer: Managers shape clarity, feedback, recognition, and psychological safety day to day.
items.explanation: People leave managers, not only companies.

items.question: Why does listening without action harm engagement programs?
items.correctAnswer: Employees learn surveys are performative, eroding trust.
items.explanation: Close the loop with visible changes.

items.question: What do whistleblower protections aim to prevent?
items.correctAnswer: Retaliation against workers who report certain legal violations in good faith.
items.explanation: Programs vary by statute and jurisdiction.

items.question: What does OSHA primarily focus on?
items.correctAnswer: Safe and healthy working conditions for employees.
items.explanation: Employers must comply with applicable safety standards.

items.question: What is a common IRS theme when classifying workers as employees vs independent contractors?
items.correctAnswer: Behavioral/financial control and relationship type—not only the label chosen.
items.explanation: Misclassification creates liability—use official guidance.

items.question: What is at-will employment (common U.S. concept)?
items.correctAnswer: Either employer or employee may end employment at any time for any lawful reason, with exceptions.
items.explanation: Exceptions include contracts, unions, and anti-discrimination protections.

items.question: What is collective bargaining?
items.correctAnswer: Negotiation between an employer and a union representing employees about wages and conditions.
items.explanation: Labor law governs representation and unfair practices.

items.question: What is the NLRB broadly responsible for?
items.correctAnswer: Enforcing private-sector labor law related to union rights and unfair labor practices (U.S.).
items.explanation: Labor relations have specialized agencies and rules.

items.question: Under U.S. FLSA basics, why does exempt vs non-exempt matter?
items.correctAnswer: Non-exempt employees generally must receive overtime pay for hours over 40 in a workweek (rules apply).
items.explanation: Classification has legal consequences—verify with counsel.

items.question: What is a clawback provision (executive comp context)?
items.correctAnswer: A contractual term allowing recovery of compensation after misconduct or restatements.
items.explanation: Clawbacks align incentives with governance.

items.question: What is a pay band?
items.correctAnswer: A defined salary range for a role or level to support consistency and equity governance.
incorrectAnswers: A fixed secret number for one person only.
items.explanation: Bands help manage internal equity and budgets.

items.question: What is pay equity work trying to reduce?
items.correctAnswer: Unexplained pay differences by gender, race, or other protected categories for similar work.
items.explanation: Analytics and governance support fair pay programs.

items.question: What is total compensation?
items.correctAnswer: Cash pay plus benefits and perks valued as part of the employment deal.
incorrectAnswers: Only base salary excluding everything else.
items.explanation: Total rewards framing improves retention conversations.

items.question: Why do benefits matter for retention?
items.correctAnswer: Health, retirement, and flexibility strongly influence whether employees stay.
items.explanation: Benefits are part of the employee value proposition.

items.question: What does SMART commonly stand for in goal setting?
items.correctAnswer: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound (variants exist).
items.explanation: SMART clarifies expectations.

items.question: What is 360-degree feedback?
items.correctAnswer: Input from peers, managers, direct reports, and sometimes customers about behaviors and impact.
items.explanation: 360s broaden perspective but need psychological safety.

items.question: What is a performance improvement plan (PIP) typically intended to do?
items.correctAnswer: Clarify gaps, expectations, support, and timeline to help an employee succeed—or document fair decisions.
items.explanation: PIPs should be fair, specific, and documented.

items.question: What is continuous performance management?
items.correctAnswer: Ongoing check-ins and feedback instead of only an annual review.
items.explanation: Frequency improves coaching and agility.

items.question: Why can forced ranking be controversial?
items.correctAnswer: It can harm collaboration, diversity, and morale if quotas distort true performance differences.
incorrectAnswers: It is universally loved with no downsides.
It is required in every country.
items.explanation: Many firms moved to continuous feedback models.

items.question: Why do companies run rating calibration sessions?
items.correctAnswer: Reduce inconsistency so similar performance gets similar ratings across managers.
items.explanation: Calibration improves fairness perceptions.

items.question: What does time-to-fill measure?
items.correctAnswer: How long it takes to fill a role from opening to acceptance (definitions vary by company).
items.explanation: Speed metrics should be balanced with quality and diversity goals.

items.question: What is employer branding?
items.correctAnswer: How candidates and employees perceive you as a place to work—shaping attraction and retention.
incorrectAnswers: Only the color of the office carpet.
items.explanation: Brand influences who applies and accepts offers.

items.question: What is sourcing in recruiting?
items.correctAnswer: Proactively identifying and engaging potential candidates—not only posting jobs.
items.explanation: Sourcing expands the candidate pool beyond applicants only.

items.question: Why is compliance important in hiring (e.g., EEO basics in the U.S.)?
items.correctAnswer: Laws and policies aim to prevent discrimination and promote fair consideration.
items.explanation: Train teams on lawful, job-related criteria.

items.question: Why track offer acceptance rate?
items.correctAnswer: It signals competitiveness of compensation, speed, and candidate experience.
items.explanation: Low acceptance may indicate misaligned offers or process drag.

items.question: Why use structured interviews?
items.correctAnswer: Consistent questions and scoring reduce bias and improve validity versus purely casual chats.
incorrectAnswers: To ask different random questions for each candidate.
items.explanation: Structure improves fairness and hiring quality.

items.question: What does the 70-20-10 model commonly suggest about learning?
items.correctAnswer: Most learning happens through experience, coaching, and formal training—not only classes.
items.explanation: Blended learning mirrors how adults develop skills.

items.question: What is Kirkpatrick Level 4 focused on?
items.correctAnswer: Business results and impact from training (after reaction, learning, behavior).
items.explanation: Impact evaluation is hardest but most valuable.

items.question: What is onboarding primarily for?
items.correctAnswer: Accelerate productivity and engagement by clarifying role, tools, culture, and expectations early.
items.explanation: Strong onboarding reduces early turnover.

items.question: What should a training needs assessment establish?
items.correctAnswer: Skill gaps, audience, and business outcomes before designing content.
items.explanation: Needs analysis prevents irrelevant training spend.

items.question: Why is certification not the same as competence?
items.correctAnswer: Certification shows completion of a standard; competence requires demonstrated performance in context.
incorrectAnswers: Certification guarantees mastery in every situation.
items.explanation: Assess on-the-job outcomes, not only credentials.

items.question: Why is training ROI hard to measure?
items.correctAnswer: Outcomes depend on many variables beyond the course itself.
items.explanation: Use aligned metrics and control for context.
